expected_outcome: K adsorption on black phosphorus introduces electron doping that reduces the band gap from ~0.3 eV (bulk) to near-zero at high coverage. The anisotropic structure of BP means the band gap modulation is direction-dependent. By analogy with Li intercalation in WTe2, heavy electron doping should trigger a structural distortion and electronic phase transition. However, BP's puckered orthorhombic structure responds differently from TMDs.
expert_explanation: Black phosphorus is not in the KG, so this requires analogical transfer from WTe2 (Li intercalation -> CDW phase transition) and MoS2 (doping -> band structure modification). The key PSP pathway is: surface doping (P) -> lattice distortion and Fermi level shift (S) -> band gap reduction and possible semiconductor-to-metal transition (P). BP's anisotropic structure means the response differs along armchair vs zigzag directions.

expected_outcome: WTe2 undergoes a pressure-induced superconducting transition. Under hydrostatic pressure, the Td phase transitions to a monoclinic structure, and superconductivity emerges below ~2K at pressures above ~2.5 GPa, with TC increasing under further compression. This follows the same PSP pattern as Li intercalation: an external perturbation (pressure instead of doping) drives a structural phase transition that creates new electronic properties.
expert_explanation: WTe2 is in the KG for Li intercalation, but pressure-induced superconductivity is not. The analogical transfer draws on: (1) Li intercalation in WTe2 induces Td -> Td' phase transition and gap opening; (2) In related TMDs like MoS2, pressure induces structural transitions and superconductivity. The PSP pathway is: high pressure (P) -> structural phase transition from Td to monoclinic (S) -> emergence of superconducting state (P).

expected_outcome: Carbon substitution at nitrogen sites in hBN creates localized mid-gap states that act as single-photon emitters. The emission wavelength depends on the local strain environment and C-C dimer configuration. By analogy with nitrogen doping effects in graphene, substitutional defects modify the local electronic structure, creating trap states that produce photoluminescence in the visible to near-IR range.
expert_explanation: hBN is not well-represented in the KG. The analogical transfer uses: (1) Nitrogen doping in graphene modifies electronic structure and carrier type; (2) Vacancy ordering in graphene affects phonon properties. For hBN, C substitution creates deep mid-gap states that serve as quantum emitters. The PSP pathway is: C substitution during CVD growth (P) -> localized defect states in the 5.9 eV band gap (S) -> single-photon emission at visible/near-IR wavelengths (P).

expected_outcome: Nb doping in PtSe2 is expected to enhance thermoelectric performance by: (1) increasing carrier concentration (p-type doping from Nb substitution at Pt sites), (2) introducing point defect scattering that reduces lattice thermal conductivity, and (3) modifying the band structure to increase the Seebeck coefficient. The figure of merit ZT should improve from ~0.1 (undoped) to potentially >0.5 at optimal Nb concentration.
expert_explanation: PtSe2 is not in the KG. Analogical transfer uses: (1) Ordered vacancy effects on thermal conductivity from graphene; (2) Dopant-induced carrier modification from MoS2 (Re doping) and WTe2 (Li intercalation). The PSP pathway is: Nb sputtering doping (P) -> substitutional defect formation and band structure modification (S) -> enhanced Seebeck coefficient and reduced thermal conductivity (P). PtSe2 has an intrinsically low thermal conductivity, making it promising for thermoelectrics.

expected_outcome: Electrostatic gating of CrI3 bilayers switches magnetic ordering between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic states. Positive gate voltage (electron doping) stabilizes ferromagnetic interlayer coupling, while negative voltage (hole doping) favors antiferromagnetic coupling. The critical voltage is ~1-2 V/nm. This follows the same PSP logic as hole-doping-induced ferromagnetism in 2D materials, but in CrI3 the dominant effect is on interlayer exchange rather than intralayer magnetism.
expert_explanation: CrI3 is not in the KG but shares key PSP features with hole-doped 2D materials. The KG contains hole-doping-induced ferromagnetism and Ising SOC phase determining magnetic exchange. By analogical transfer: gate voltage (P) modifies electron density in CrI3 layers (S), which controls the interlayer exchange coupling from AFM to FM (P). This is a clean example of how processing (electrostatic gating) controls structure (interlayer magnetic coupling) to determine properties (net magnetization).
